•  Column statistics and file pruning from Delta’s transaction log accelerate your T-SQL queries the same way they accelerate Spark queries.
You can access the endpoint from three places:
Fabric portal — Open the Lakehouse, click the dropdown at the top-right, and switch to “SQL analytics endpoint” view. A built-in SQL editor appears where you can write and run queries.

SSMS / Azure Data Studio / DBeaver — The endpoint has a standard TDS (Tabular Data Stream) connection string. Find it in the Lakehouse settings or the SQL endpoint item in your workspace. Connect using Microsoft Entra ID authentication (your organizational account).
JDBC / ODBC clients — Any application that supports JDBC or ODBC can connect: Excel, Power BI Desktop (via SQL endpoint connection), Python with pyodbc, Java applications.
Auth:     Microsoft Entra ID (Azure Active Directory)
-- In SSMS: Server name = the endpoint URL
-- Authentication = Azure Active Directory - Universal with MFA
Part 3: Querying Energy Data with T-SQL

The SQL analytics endpoint supports the full T-SQL SELECT syntax. Everything you know about T-SQL — JOINs, CTEs, window functions, aggregations, subqueries, CASE expressions — works here. The only restriction is that you cannot write data.
JOIN dim_substations s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
-- Substation load analysis: daily → monthly → classify
JOIN dim_substations s ON ms.substation_id = s.substation_id
-- Day-over-day and week-over-week comparison per meter
daily_kwh - LAG(daily_kwh, 1) OVER w AS daily_change,
PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY reading_date
ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY reading_date)
-- Rank substations by consumption within each region
PARTITION BY region ORDER BY SUM(daily_kwh) DESC
Part 4: Creating Views for Governed Analytics

Views are your primary tool for building reusable, governed analytical abstractions on top of Lakehouse tables. A view stores a SQL definition (not data) and executes it each time the view is queried. Views in the SQL analytics endpoint are persistent — they survive across sessions and are visible to all SQL clients and Power BI.
Abstraction — Analysts query v_daily_grid_summary instead of writing a 30-line GROUP BY with 5 JOINs. The complexity is encapsulated in the view.
Governance — You can grant SELECT on the view without granting access to the underlying tables. Analysts see only what the view exposes.
Consistency — When the calculation for ‘peak demand’ changes, you update one view definition. Every report and query that uses the view gets the corrected logic automatically.
Power BI integration — Views appear in Power BI’s connection dialog alongside tables. They are the recommended way to shape data for semantic models.
-- Daily grid summary for operations dashboards
LEFT JOIN dim_tariffs t ON m.tariff_code = t.tariff_code
-- Substation health view for asset management
PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY reading_date
ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW

These three views provide: a grid operations summary (for dashboards), a substation health assessment (for asset management), and an anomaly feed (for automated alerting). They are the governed abstractions that downstream consumers rely on.

One of Fabric’s most powerful features for SQL analysts is the ability to query across Lakehouses and Warehouses using three-part naming. This eliminates the need to copy data for cross-domain analysis.
-- Query a table in the current Lakehouse (no prefix)
-- Query another Lakehouse in the same workspace
-- Query a Warehouse in the same workspace
-- Cross-item join: Gold Lakehouse meters + Warehouse aggregates
JOIN wh_grid_analytics.dbo.dim_regions w ON m.region = w.region_code

Cross-database queries work in both directions: you can join Lakehouse tables to Warehouse tables and vice versa. The SQL engine resolves the references and reads from OneLake transparently. This means a SQL analyst sitting in the Warehouse can read the Gold Lakehouse tables without needing Spark access, and an analyst using the Lakehouse endpoint can read Warehouse summary tables.
Part 6: The Default Semantic Model and DirectLake

Every Lakehouse automatically generates a Power BI semantic model in DirectLake mode. DirectLake is Fabric’s native connection mode: Power BI reads Delta Parquet files directly from OneLake into its in-memory engine, combining the speed of Import mode with the freshness of DirectQuery.
What DirectLake Means for SQL Analysts

When you create a view in the SQL analytics endpoint (v_daily_grid_summary), that view’s underlying tables are available in the default semantic model. A BI developer can build a Power BI report directly on Lakehouse tables or on a custom semantic model connected via DirectLake — without importing data or scheduling refreshes. The data the report shows is always the latest version of the Delta tables.

DirectLake is the recommended mode for all Lakehouse-based Power BI reports on the energy platform. The Grid Operations Dashboard reads agg_daily_grid_summary via DirectLake and sees the latest data as soon as the morning pipeline completes.
Part 7: Limitations of the SQL Analytics Endpoint
The SQL analytics endpoint is read-only for Lakehouse tables. Understanding what you cannot do is as important as knowing what you can.

The key decision: If you only need to read and analyze data, the SQL analytics endpoint is sufficient. If you need to write data, create stored procedures, or encapsulate logic in T-SQL procedures, you need a Fabric Data Warehouse (Module DW2). Many platforms use both: the Lakehouse endpoint for ad-hoc analytics on Silver/Gold tables, and the Warehouse for T-SQL-driven Gold layer transformations.
Part 8: Lakehouse Endpoint vs. Warehouse — Decision Framework

Important: There is no wrong answer. Both items store Delta tables in OneLake. Both feed Power BI. The choice is about which SQL capabilities your workflow requires. In many energy organizations, the answer is both — Lakehouses for the engineering pipeline, the Warehouse for governed T-SQL analytics and procedural logic.
The SQL analytics endpoint supports T-SQL security commands for controlling analyst access at the table, column, and row level.
-- Grant SELECT on Gold tables to the analyst group
-- Deny access to Bronze tables (analysts should not see raw data)
RLS restricts which rows an analyst can see based on their identity. For the energy platform, this typically means analysts only see data for their assigned grid region:
-- Create a mapping table: user → region
-- (In practice, this table is maintained by the admin team)
-- Apply the policy to the meters table
ADD FILTER PREDICATE dbo.fn_region_filter(region) ON dbo.meters

With this policy active, north.analyst@grid.com sees only NORTH region meters when querying the meters table. The same policy can be applied to views and to the Power BI semantic model for consistent security across all consumers.
Part 10: Best Practices for SQL Analysts on Lakehouses

•  Query Gold-layer tables, not Bronze or Silver. Gold tables are deduplicated, typed, validated, and modeled for analytics. Bronze and Silver contain raw or intermediate data that may confuse analysts.
•  Create views for every recurring query. If you write the same GROUP BY + JOIN three times, create a view. Views are the SQL analyst’s primary contribution to platform governance.
•  Use three-part naming for cross-item joins. This lets you combine data from multiple Lakehouses and Warehouses without data copying.
•  Leverage window functions for time-series analysis. LAG, LEAD, moving averages, and running totals are essential for energy analytics and are fully supported.
•  Use CTEs for multi-step analytics. They make complex queries readable, debuggable, and maintainable.

•  Connect from SSMS or Azure Data Studio for serious development. The in-portal SQL editor is fine for quick queries, but external tools provide IntelliSense, query plans, and better session management.

•  Communicate data needs to the engineering team. If you need a column that does not exist in the Gold layer, or if a data quality issue affects your analysis, create a backlog item. The engineering pipeline is the right place to fix data, not a SQL workaround.
•  Document your views. Add comments in the CREATE VIEW statement explaining the business purpose, expected consumers, and any assumptions.
•  The Lakehouse from a SQL perspective — Tables/ as your queryable data, the SQL analytics endpoint as your interface, Files/ as the engineer’s domain
•  The SQL analytics endpoint — auto-generated, read-only T-SQL interface; connection strings; accessing from SSMS, Azure Data Studio, JDBC/ODBC
•  T-SQL querying — SELECT, JOINs, CTEs, window functions (LAG, LEAD, moving averages, RANK), aggregations — all on Delta tables in OneLake
•  Views — persistent views for governed analytics (v_daily_grid_summary, v_substation_health, v_meter_anomalies); views as the analyst’s governance tool
•  Cross-database queries — three-part naming for joining Lakehouse tables to Warehouse tables and vice versa
•  DirectLake and the semantic model — Power BI reads Delta directly from OneLake; no import, no refresh; near-import performance with always-current data
•  Limitations — no DML, no stored procedures, no CREATE TABLE, no Delta maintenance; understanding what requires a Warehouse
•  Decision framework — Lakehouse endpoint for reads and views, Warehouse for writes and procedures, both for mixed teams
•  Security — GRANT/DENY on tables and views, Row-Level Security with security policies and filter predicates
•  Best practices — query Gold not Bronze, create views, use CTEs and windows, connect external tools, communicate data needs to engineers
Before moving to Module DW2, complete these exercises:
•  Open the SQL analytics endpoint for a Lakehouse with energy data (lh_gold_grid or your workshop Lakehouse). Identify the available tables.
•  Write a T-SQL query that calculates daily consumption by region with COUNT, SUM, AVG, and MAX. Use a WHERE clause to filter to the last 30 days.
•  Write a CTE-based query: aggregate daily consumption by substation (Step 1), classify by load level (Step 2), and summarize by classification (Step 3).
•  Write a window function query: add a 7-day moving average per meter, a LAG for day-over-day change, and a RANK within region. Use the named WINDOW clause.
•  Create a persistent view (v_daily_grid_summary) that encapsulates the regional summary query. Verify it is visible from the SQL analytics endpoint UI.
•  Create a second view (v_substation_health) that calculates substation age and maintenance status.
•  Connect to the SQL analytics endpoint from SSMS or Azure Data Studio. Run one of your queries from the external tool.
•  If a Warehouse exists in the workspace, write a cross-database query that joins a Lakehouse table to a Warehouse table using three-part naming.
You are not just querying tables.

You are building governed analytical abstractions — views, CTEs, and cross-database joins — that turn raw Delta tables into trusted energy intelligence. The SQL analytics endpoint gives you the full power of T-SQL over a modern lakehouse platform, without needing Spark, Python, or notebooks.

In Module DW2, we move to the Fabric Data Warehouse — the T-SQL-native analytical engine with full DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE), stored procedures, schemas, and fine-grained security. You will learn when and how to use the Warehouse for T-SQL-driven data engineering and governed Gold-layer transformations.

rows: Yesterday’s meter data until next scheduled refresh.
Real-time but every slicer click hits the SQL engine.
Latest meter readings, no refresh needed, fast interactivity.
SELECT queries (JOINs, CTEs, window functions, aggregations)
Use Spark Notebooks or a Warehouse for DML.
Tables are created by Spark, Pipelines, or Dataflows.
Use a Warehouse for procedural T-SQL.
T-SQL security (GRANT SELECT on specific tables)
Use Pipelines or a SQL Database.
Ad-hoc queries on Gold-layer meter data
Read-only is fine. Data is already prepared by the engineering pipeline.
Creating reusable views for Power BI
Views are supported and persist. DirectLake works directly.
Running stored procedures for billing calculations
Stored procedures require the Warehouse’s full T-SQL engine.
Building a T-SQL-driven Gold layer (MERGE, INSERT)
DML operations are only available in the Warehouse.
SQL analysts who need write access
The Lakehouse endpoint is read-only by design.
Cross-database joins between Lakehouse and Warehouse
Regulatory audit queries on historical data
Time travel (VERSION AS OF) is available via Spark, but the endpoint provides the latest snapshot for audit views.
Mixed team: some engineers (PySpark), some analysts (T-SQL)
Engineers use Lakehouses; analysts use the SQL endpoint for reads and the Warehouse for writes.

blocks.content: Module Objective: Build and operate a Fabric Data Warehouse for energy analytics — schemas for organizational clarity, T-SQL DDL for table management, data loading with COPY INTO, full DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE), stored procedures for encapsulated transformation logic, views and functions, security at every level, cross-database queries to Lakehouses, monitoring and performance, and automatic table maintenance.

Module DW1 taught you the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint — a read-only T-SQL window into Delta tables. You can query, create views, and run analytics. But you cannot write data, create stored procedures, or encapsulate transformation logic in T-SQL.

For SQL teams that manage the energy grid’s analytical layer, read-only access is not enough. You need to INSERT staging data, UPDATE substation attributes, DELETE expired records, MERGE daily meter loads into fact tables, and wrap all of that logic in stored procedures that Pipelines can call. You need schemas to separate staging from gold. You need fine-grained security to control who can write and who can only read.

The Fabric Data Warehouse gives you all of this. It is a fully managed, T-SQL-native analytical engine that stores Delta tables in OneLake. It provides the full T-SQL surface that SQL professionals expect: CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, stored procedures, views, functions, schemas, and fine-grained permissions.

The critical insight: the Warehouse and the Lakehouse store data in the same format (Delta) in the same place (OneLake). They are two interfaces to one storage layer. A table created in a Warehouse is a Delta table in OneLake. A Spark notebook can read it. A Lakehouse can shortcut to it. The data is the same. Only the access interface differs.

This module covers: what the Warehouse is and how it differs from the Lakehouse, creating a Warehouse and organizing with schemas, T-SQL DDL for energy table creation, data loading with COPY INTO from OneLake files, full DML (UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE), stored procedures for encapsulated grid data transformations, views and user-defined functions, security at schema/table/column/row level, cross-database queries to Lakehouses, monitoring and performance, automatic table maintenance, and a comprehensive checkpoint.
Part 1: What Is the Fabric Data Warehouse?

The Fabric Data Warehouse is a fully managed, T-SQL-native analytical engine that stores data as Delta tables in OneLake. It provides the complete T-SQL surface that SQL professionals expect, without the infrastructure management of traditional SQL Server or Azure SQL.
Warehouse vs. Lakehouse — The Complete Comparison

Important: The storage is identical. Both items write Delta tables to OneLake. The difference is entirely in the interface: which SQL dialect, which write mechanisms, which procedural capabilities. For your energy platform, choose based on your team’s skills and workflow requirements, not based on storage architecture.
Part 2: Creating a Warehouse and Organizing with Schemas

Creating a Warehouse is as simple as creating a Lakehouse: navigate to your workspace, click “+ New item” → “Warehouse,” enter a name (wh_grid_analytics), and click Create. The Warehouse appears within seconds.

Unlike the Lakehouse, there is no Files/ section — the Warehouse is a pure table store. You interact with it through the built-in SQL editor in the Fabric portal, or through any T-SQL client (SSMS, Azure Data Studio, DBeaver).
Schemas separate tables into logical groups. The default schema is dbo, but you should create additional schemas to separate concerns on the energy platform:
-- Create schemas for the energy grid warehouse
CREATE SCHEMA staging;   -- Raw/intermediate data from Pipelines
CREATE SCHEMA gold;      -- Business-ready star schema tables
CREATE SCHEMA audit;     -- Data quality logs, refresh history

Schemas are a governance mechanism. You can grant permissions at the schema level: analysts get SELECT on gold but no access to staging or audit. This is a fundamental advantage over the Lakehouse endpoint, where granular SQL-level schema separation is not available.

The Warehouse opens in a SQL editor within the Fabric portal with IntelliSense (auto-complete for table names, column names, T-SQL keywords), multiple query tabs, a result grid with sorting and export, execution plan visualization, and an object explorer showing schemas, tables, views, procedures, and functions. For serious development, connect from SSMS or Azure Data Studio using the Warehouse’s TDS connection string.
Part 3: T-SQL DDL — Creating Energy Tables
-- Staging table for raw meter readings from Pipeline Copy Activity
-- Gold fact table for meter readings
-- Gold dimension table for substations (SCD Type 2)
CASE WHEN DATEPART(WEEKDAY, d) IN (1, 7) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_weekend
-- Add a tariff column to the fact table
ALTER TABLE gold.fact_meter_readings ADD tariff_code VARCHAR(10);
-- Drop a staging table after processing

Important: The Warehouse does not support ALTER COLUMN to change data types. To change a column’s type, create a new table with the desired schema and move the data with INSERT...SELECT. This is a deliberate trade-off for the distributed Delta storage engine.
Part 4: Loading Data — COPY INTO, INSERT, and Pipelines
COPY INTO is the fastest way to load data into a Warehouse table. It reads files from OneLake and bulk-loads them in a single optimized operation.
-- Load meter readings CSV from a Lakehouse Files/ section

COPY INTO parallelizes the read across multiple files and uses bulk insert mechanics. For initial loads of millions of meter readings, it is significantly faster than INSERT...SELECT from an external source.
-- Transform staging into gold fact table
JOIN gold.dim_meters m ON stg.meter_id = m.meter_id AND m.is_current = 1
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON stg.substation_id = s.substation_id AND s.is_current = 1
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON stg.reading_date = d.calendar_date
LEFT JOIN gold.dim_tariffs t ON m.tariff_code = t.tariff_code
Pipelines can load data into the Warehouse through:
Copy Activity — Move data from any of 300+ supported sources directly into a Warehouse staging table. The most common pattern for energy platforms: SFTP → staging table.
Dataflow Gen2 — Configure a Warehouse table as the destination for a Power Query transformation. Useful for analyst-managed reference data.

Stored Procedure Activity — Call a Warehouse stored procedure from a Pipeline to execute T-SQL transformation logic. This is the cleanest integration: Pipeline lands data with Copy Activity, then calls a stored procedure for MERGE into gold.
Part 5: T-SQL DML — UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE
Full DML is the primary advantage over the Lakehouse’s read-only SQL endpoint. You can modify data directly with familiar T-SQL syntax.
-- Update with a JOIN: recalculate estimated cost after tariff change
JOIN gold.dim_meters m ON f.meter_sk = m.meter_sk
JOIN gold.dim_tariffs t ON m.tariff_code = t.tariff_code
-- Data retention: remove staging data older than 30 days
-- Remove orphaned substations not referenced by any meter reading
-- Daily meter reading upsert: insert new, update corrections
WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN

T-SQL MERGE syntax note: T-SQL uses WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET (vs. Spark SQL’s WHEN NOT MATCHED). T-SQL MERGE requires a semicolon terminator. Otherwise, the logic is identical: insert new rows, update existing rows, optionally delete. The same four MERGE patterns from Module DE6 (simple upsert, SCD1, idempotent fact, conditional delete) apply here with T-SQL syntax.

Stored procedures are the Warehouse’s most significant capability advantage over the Lakehouse. They encapsulate T-SQL logic into reusable, parameterized units that can be called from Pipelines, from other procedures, or interactively.
A Production Stored Procedure: Refresh Meter Readings
-- Step 1: MERGE staging into gold fact table
JOIN gold.dim_meters m ON stg.meter_id = m.meter_id AND m.is_current = 1
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON stg.substation_id = s.substation_id AND s.is_current = 1
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON stg.reading_date = d.calendar_date
LEFT JOIN gold.dim_tariffs tr ON m.tariff_code = tr.tariff_code
ON t.meter_sk = src.meter_sk AND t.date_key = src.date_key
WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN INSERT
INSERT INTO audit.refresh_log (table_name, load_date, row_count, status, executed_at)
-- From a Pipeline: use the Stored Procedure Activity
Encapsulation — All logic for refreshing meter readings is in one place. If the MERGE logic or cost calculation changes, you update the procedure, not every pipeline that references it.
Parameterization — Procedures accept parameters (@load_date, @grid_region, @mode), making them reusable across different pipeline runs and scenarios.
Audit logging — The procedure logs every execution to audit.refresh_log with row counts and status. This creates an audit trail for regulatory compliance.

Pipeline integration — The Pipeline Stored Procedure Activity calls a procedure directly, passing parameters from the pipeline. Pipeline lands data → procedure MERGEs it — the cleanest T-SQL automation pattern.

Warehouse views serve the same governance purpose as Lakehouse endpoint views (DW1), but with the additional advantage of being able to reference stored procedures’ output tables and DML-maintained Gold tables.
-- Monthly regional summary for executive dashboards
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON f.substation_sk = s.substation_sk
-- ETL service principal: full access to staging and gold
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON SCHEMA::staging TO [ETLServicePrincipal];
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON SCHEMA::gold TO [ETLServicePrincipal];
-- Billing team: execute procedure only (cannot write arbitrary SQL)
-- Security function: filter by user's assigned region
-- Join Warehouse gold tables with Lakehouse Silver tables

Cross-database queries enable a federated architecture: the Warehouse owns the Gold star schema, while the Lakehouse owns Silver-layer data managed by data engineers. Analysts in the Warehouse can join across both without data duplication.
-- Find the slowest queries in the last 24 hours
•  Write selective queries — always include WHERE clauses. Delta’s file-level statistics enable pruning, but only if you filter.
•  Avoid SELECT * — specify only needed columns. Delta’s columnar format skips unread columns entirely.
•  Use CTAS for full rebuilds — CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT is more efficient than INSERT INTO ... SELECT for complete table refreshes.
•  Prefer MERGE over separate UPDATE + INSERT — MERGE processes the source once and applies all changes atomically.
•  Monitor with queryinsights — set alerts for queries exceeding expected thresholds.
Unlike the Lakehouse (where you manually run OPTIMIZE and VACUUM via Spark SQL), the Warehouse handles table maintenance automatically:
Automatic file compaction — The Warehouse engine periodically compacts small Delta files into larger ones. You do not need to run OPTIMIZE.
Automatic statistics management — The engine maintains column statistics for query optimization. You do not need to create or update statistics.
V-Order applied by default — All Warehouse writes use V-Order optimization for optimal read performance across all Fabric engines.

Operational impact: Automatic maintenance means you spend less time managing the Warehouse compared to a Lakehouse. For SQL teams without dedicated data engineering staff for OPTIMIZE and VACUUM schedules, this is a significant operational advantage.
•  What the Warehouse is — T-SQL-native analytical engine storing Delta tables in OneLake; same storage as Lakehouse, different interface; 12-point comparison table
•  Schemas — staging, gold, audit for organizational clarity; schema-level permissions for governance
•  T-SQL DDL — CREATE TABLE with energy schemas, data type reference, dim_date generation with GENERATE_SERIES, ALTER/DROP/RENAME
•  Data loading — COPY INTO for bulk file loads, INSERT...SELECT for in-Warehouse transforms, Pipeline integration (Copy Activity + Stored Procedure Activity), 5-method comparison table
•  Full DML — UPDATE with JOINs (tariff recalculation), DELETE with retention policies, MERGE for daily meter upserts; T-SQL syntax differences from Spark SQL
•  Stored procedures — usp_refresh_meter_readings with parameterized MERGE, audit logging, Pipeline Stored Procedure Activity integration; UDFs for reusable calculations
•  Views — gold.v_monthly_grid_summary for executive dashboards; views on Warehouse tables with full DML-maintained data
•  Security — 6 layers (workspace, schema, table, column, row, procedure); schema-level GRANT/DENY for team separation; Row-Level Security for regional access
•  Cross-database queries — Warehouse ↔ Lakehouse joins via three-part naming; federated architecture patterns
•  Monitoring and maintenance — queryinsights DMVs, performance best practices, automatic compaction/statistics/V-Order
Before moving to Module DW3, complete these exercises:
•  Create a Warehouse (wh_grid_analytics). Create three schemas: staging, gold, audit.
•  Create staging.raw_meter_readings with an explicit schema matching your energy data. Use COPY INTO to load a CSV or Parquet file from your Lakehouse’s Files/ section.
•  Create gold.dim_date using the GENERATE_SERIES pattern (2020–2030).
•  Create gold.dim_substations with SCD Type 2 columns (effective_from, effective_to, is_current).
•  Create gold.fact_meter_readings. Write an INSERT...SELECT that transforms staging data into the fact table with surrogate key resolution and cost calculation.
•  Write a MERGE statement that upserts staging meter readings into the fact table: insert new readings, update corrections. Run it twice — verify idempotency.
•  Create stored procedure gold.usp_refresh_meter_readings with parameterized @load_date, MERGE logic, and audit logging. Call it with EXEC.
•  Set up security: GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA::gold TO an analyst group. DENY on staging. Verify the analyst can query gold but not staging.
•  Write a cross-database query joining your Warehouse fact table with a Lakehouse Silver table. Verify the join works.
•  Query queryinsights.exec_requests_history to find your slowest query. Examine what could be optimized.
You now command the full T-SQL analytical engine.

In Module DW3, we go deep into T-SQL Analytics — advanced window functions with frame specifications, CTEs and recursive hierarchies, PIVOT/UNPIVOT for cross-tab reports, JSON handling for semi-structured energy data, SCD Type 2 patterns in pure T-SQL, and the production query patterns that separate a database user from an analytics engineer.

rows: No — all data must be in tables
Manual: OPTIMIZE, VACUUM via Spark SQL
SQL analysts, analytics engineers, BI developers
INT for surrogate keys (meter_sk, substation_sk). BIGINT for very large fact tables.
DECIMAL(12,3) for daily_kwh. DECIMAL(12,2) for costs/prices. Always specify precision and scale.
FLOAT for SCADA sensor values (wind_speed_ms, rotor_rpm). Use DECIMAL for financial amounts.
VARCHAR(20) for meter_id, region. VARCHAR(100) for substation_name. Fabric uses UTF-8 (no NVARCHAR).
DATE for reading_date, commission_date. DATETIME2 for event timestamps. Avoid legacy DATETIME.
Bulk loading files from OneLake/ADLS Gen2
Load 2M meter reading CSVs from Files/landing/ into staging table
Transform staging into gold with dimension key resolution
Loading from external sources (SQL, REST, SaaS)
Load energy pricing from Azure SQL trading database
Call usp_refresh_fact_meters after staging is loaded
Tariff schedules from SharePoint Excel into gold.dim_tariffs
Engineers as Members (write), analysts as Viewers (read)
GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA::gold TO [GridAnalysts]. DENY ON SCHEMA::staging.
North region analysts see only NORTH meter readings
Billing team can EXEC usp_refresh_meter_readings but cannot write arbitrary SQL

blocks.content: Module Objective: Master advanced T-SQL patterns for energy analytics — window function frame specifications, CROSS APPLY for correlated subqueries, recursive CTEs for grid hierarchies, PIVOT/UNPIVOT for cross-tab reports, JSON handling for weather API data, OPENROWSET for querying files without loading, SCD Type 2 in pure T-SQL, query performance tuning, and the production pattern library that separates a database user from an analytics engineer.

Module DW2 gave you the Warehouse: the T-SQL-native engine with full DDL, DML, stored procedures, and security. You can create tables, load data, write MERGE statements, and govern access. That is the infrastructure.

This module is about what you build on that infrastructure. Advanced T-SQL patterns are what separate a database user from an analytics engineer. Window functions with precise frame specifications for running grid load calculations. CROSS APPLY to get the top 3 peak readings per substation. Recursive CTEs for the grid’s feeder hierarchy. PIVOT for regional cross-tab reports. JSON parsing for weather API responses. OPENROWSET for querying meter files without loading them. SCD Type 2 for tracking substation attribute changes over time.

The single most important skill in this module is knowing which pattern to reach for. When you see a problem — unnest weather JSON, compare this month to last year, deduplicate overlapping meter exports, build a grid topology hierarchy — the right pattern should come to mind immediately. This module gives you that pattern library.

These patterns apply to all three SQL surfaces in Fabric: the Warehouse (full DML), the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint (read-only), and the SQL Database (OLTP). The T-SQL syntax is identical. The difference is which surface supports which write operations.

DW1 introduced basic window functions (LAG, RANK, moving averages). This section goes deeper into frame specifications, the ROWS vs. RANGE distinction, and advanced patterns for energy time-series analysis.

Warning: The default frame when ORDER BY is specified is RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW — not ROWS. This means ties are grouped together, which produces incorrect results for SUM and AVG on energy time-series data (where two readings rarely share the exact same date, but if they do, the total would be wrong). Always specify ROWS explicitly when you want physical row counting.
Advanced Window Patterns for Energy Analytics
ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
-- Month-over-month comparison using LAG(1) on monthly aggregates
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON f.substation_sk = s.substation_sk
PARTITION BY region ORDER BY calendar_year, calendar_month
PARTITION BY region ORDER BY calendar_year, calendar_month
PARTITION BY region ORDER BY calendar_year, calendar_month
PARTITION BY region ORDER BY calendar_year, calendar_month
-- Percent of region total (no ORDER BY = entire partition)
-- Gap detection: find missing reading dates per meter
PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY reading_date
PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY reading_date
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key;
-- Filter WHERE gap_days > 1 to find missing days
-- First and last reading per meter in a billing period
PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY reading_date
ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY reading_date
ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key

Important: LAST_VALUE requires an explicit frame extending to UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING. Without it, the default frame stops at CURRENT ROW, and LAST_VALUE just returns the current row’s value — almost never what you want.
Part 2: CROSS APPLY and OUTER APPLY
APPLY is T-SQL’s operator for correlated subqueries that return multiple rows. It evaluates a table-valued expression for each row of the outer table. Think of it as a “for-each join.”
Top N Per Group — Energy Examples
-- For each substation, get its top 5 highest daily readings
JOIN gold.dim_meters m ON f.meter_sk = m.meter_sk
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
-- OUTER APPLY: include substations even if no readings exist
-- (useful for finding inactive or newly commissioned substations)
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key

CROSS APPLY is like INNER JOIN for table-valued expressions — substations without readings are excluded. OUTER APPLY is like LEFT JOIN — substations without readings produce NULLs. OUTER APPLY is essential for finding substations that have no recent readings (potential data quality or commissioning issue).
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON f.substation_sk = s.substation_sk
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
GROUP BY substation_id, region, voltage_class, calendar_month
Power grids have a hierarchical topology: substations feed from transmission points through intermediate distribution points. A recursive CTE traverses this hierarchy:
-- Grid feeder hierarchy: from transmission root to distribution leaf
WHERE parent_substation_id IS NULL AND is_current = 1
-- Recursive: substations fed by a parent in the previous level
JOIN grid_tree p ON c.parent_substation_id = p.substation_id
SELECT * FROM grid_tree ORDER BY path;

This produces a flattened view of the grid topology with depth levels and a human-readable path. Useful for: identifying all substations downstream of a failing transmission point, calculating total load at any level of the hierarchy, and building treemap visualizations in Power BI.
Part 4: PIVOT, UNPIVOT, and Dynamic Crosstabs
-- Monthly consumption with regions as columns
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON f.substation_sk = s.substation_sk
-- Source: turbine generation report with monthly columns
-- When new regions might appear (e.g., CENTRAL added)
FROM (SELECT DISTINCT region FROM gold.dim_substations WHERE is_current = 1) r;
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON f.substation_sk = s.substation_sk

Warning: Dynamic SQL (sp_executesql) is powerful but carries SQL injection risk. Always use QUOTENAME() around values injected into column identifiers. Never concatenate user-supplied strings directly into dynamic SQL.
Part 5: JSON and Semi-Structured Energy Data

Weather API responses, SCADA event payloads, and IoT device registries frequently arrive as JSON. T-SQL in Fabric provides native functions for parsing, querying, and transforming JSON directly in the Warehouse.
-- Weather API response stored in staging.raw_weather_json
-- Flatten nested array into relational rows

OPENJSON with a WITH clause maps JSON properties to typed relational columns. Combined with CROSS APPLY, it unnests one JSON array into one row per forecast hour — turning a single weather API document with 48 hourly forecasts into 48 relational rows that can be joined to meter reading dates for demand/weather correlation analysis.
Part 6: OPENROWSET — Querying Files Without Loading

OPENROWSET lets you query files directly from T-SQL without loading them into a table first. The SQL engine reads Parquet, CSV, or Delta files from OneLake and returns them as a relational result set.
-- Query Parquet meter files from a Lakehouse Files/ section
-- Query Delta table directly (useful for exploring Lakehouse tables)
-- Create a view over external files (no ETL needed for exploration)
daily_kwh DECIMAL(12,3), peak_kw DECIMAL(10,3), region VARCHAR(20)

When to use OPENROWSET: data exploration (preview before loading), ad-hoc federated queries (join files with Warehouse tables), and views over files for low-frequency analyst access. For high-frequency queries, load data with COPY INTO for better performance — OPENROWSET reads files on every execution with no caching.
Part 7: SCD Type 2 in Pure T-SQL

SCD Type 2 is the history-preserving pattern: when a substation’s voltage or region changes, you expire the current row and insert a new row with the updated value. This preserves the full history for regulatory audit and point-in-time analytics.
The SCD2 T-SQL Pattern for dim_substations
-- Step 2: Expire current rows for changed substations
WHERE substation_id IN (SELECT substation_id FROM changes)
-- Step 3a: Insert new versions for changed substations
SELECT substation_id, substation_name, voltage_kv, region, status,
-- Point-in-time: what was the substation's region on 2025-06-15?
-- Historical fact join: each reading gets the substation state
-- that was current when the reading was taken
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON f.substation_sk = s.substation_sk;
-- Because the fact table stores surrogate keys at load time,
-- each reading automatically joins to the correct historical version.
The Warehouse SQL editor’s “Explain” button shows the estimated execution plan. Key operators to understand:
Table Scan / Full Scan — The engine reads every file. If your WHERE clause should filter data, check for functions on filter columns or implicit type conversions that prevent pushdown.
Shuffle (Exchange) — Data is redistributed across nodes for joins or aggregations. Expensive. Minimize by structuring joins on natural keys.
Broadcast Move — A small table is broadcast to all nodes. Efficient for dim_substations (50K rows), problematic if a large table is broadcast by mistake.
Part 9: Set Operations for Energy Data
-- EXCEPT: Find substations with no readings this month
SELECT substation_id FROM gold.dim_substations WHERE is_current = 1
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON f.substation_sk = s.substation_sk
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
-- INTERSECT: Substations that are both high-voltage AND high-load
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON f.substation_sk = s.substation_sk
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
-- UNION ALL: Combine meter and SCADA alerts into one feed
SELECT 'METER' AS source, meter_id AS entity_id,
reading_date AS event_date, 'High consumption' AS alert_type
Wrap SCD2 in a Stored Procedure
-- Encapsulate the entire SCD2 pattern from Part 7:
-- Check that meter data arrived today
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key;
You now have the advanced T-SQL pattern library for energy analytics:

•  Window function masterclass — ROWS vs. RANGE frames, running YTD totals, month-over-month and YoY comparisons with LAG, percent of region total, gap detection with LEAD, FIRST_VALUE/LAST_VALUE with explicit frames
•  CROSS APPLY and OUTER APPLY — Top 5 readings per substation, finding substations with no recent readings (OUTER APPLY for data quality monitoring)
•  CTEs and recursion — 4-step energy CTE (daily → monthly → classify → summarize), recursive CTE for grid feeder hierarchy with depth and path
•  PIVOT and UNPIVOT — regional consumption cross-tab, normalizing columnar generation reports, dynamic PIVOT with STRING_AGG for unknown regions
•  JSON handling — JSON_VALUE for scalar extraction, OPENJSON + CROSS APPLY for flattening weather API arrays into relational rows, typed WITH clause, FOR JSON PATH
•  OPENROWSET — querying Parquet/CSV/Delta files from OneLake without loading, views over external files for exploration, when to use vs. COPY INTO
•  SCD Type 2 in T-SQL — identify changes CTE, expire old rows, insert new versions, insert brand-new substations; point-in-time queries; historical fact joins
•  Performance tuning — reading execution plans, 6 anti-patterns with fixes (functions on filters, implicit casts, unnecessary ORDER BY)
•  Set operations — EXCEPT for finding missing substations, INTERSECT for multi-criteria filtering, UNION ALL for combined alert feeds
•  Production patterns — deduplication in procedures, SCD2 as a stored procedure, data freshness checks

•  Write a window function query with explicit ROWS frame: running YTD consumption per meter, 7-day moving average, and LAG for day-over-day change. Compare ROWS vs. RANGE on the running total to observe the difference.
•  Write a CROSS APPLY query: for each substation, get its top 3 highest daily readings. Write the same query with OUTER APPLY and identify substations with no readings.
•  Write a 3+ step CTE: aggregate daily → monthly → classify → summarize for substation load analysis.
•  Write a PIVOT query: monthly consumption with regions as columns. Then write the Dynamic PIVOT version using STRING_AGG.
•  Parse a JSON weather payload: use OPENJSON + CROSS APPLY to flatten hourly forecasts into relational rows with typed columns.
•  Query Lakehouse files with OPENROWSET: read Parquet meter files without loading them. Create a view over the OPENROWSET query.
•  Implement SCD Type 2 for dim_substations in pure T-SQL: simulate a voltage change, verify the old row is expired and the new row is current. Query the point-in-time state for a past date.
•  Examine a slow query’s execution plan. Identify whether a function on a filter column is preventing file pruning. Rewrite the filter to enable pushdown.

In Module DW4, we introduce the SQL Database in Fabric — the OLTP engine with automatic replication to OneLake. You will learn when transactional workloads need a SQL Database instead of a Warehouse, how zero-ETL replication works, and how the three SQL items (Lakehouse endpoint, Warehouse, SQL Database) form a complete SQL architecture.

rows: ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
Running total of daily_kwh per meter. Each row adds exactly once.
RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW

If two readings share the same date, both are included in each other’s total (usually not what you want).

Centered moving average for smoothing SCADA noise.

Test if a string is valid JSON
Parse JSON into a relational rowset

Reads all columns. Delta skips unreferenced columns for free.
Function on column prevents file pruning. Engine cannot use min/max stats.
Implicit type conversion on every row prevents pushdown.
Ensure join columns have matching types.

Remove ORDER BY unless consumer requires it. Use TOP N.

blocks.content: OLTP Meets Analytics — The Zero-ETL Vision

Module Objective: Understand Fabric’s SQL Database — the OLTP engine for transactional energy workloads. Learn normalized schema design for meter registration and work order management, CRUD operations with ACID transactions, automatic replication to OneLake, cross-workload querying, stored procedures and triggers, and how the three SQL items (Lakehouse endpoint, Warehouse, SQL Database) form a complete architecture.

Modules DW2 and DW3 taught you the Warehouse — Fabric’s analytical SQL engine designed for star schemas, aggregations, and large scans. The Warehouse is not designed for the other half of the SQL world: transactional workloads.

Transactional workloads on an energy platform look different from analytical workloads. A meter registration system inserts one meter at a time. A work order management application updates a single maintenance task’s status. A field crew’s mobile app looks up a substation by ID. These are OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) operations: small, fast, point reads and writes with strict ACID guarantees. The Warehouse’s distributed columnar engine is not optimized for this pattern.

Fabric’s SQL Database fills this gap. It is a fully managed SQL Server database running inside Fabric — the same engine that powers Azure SQL Database, with the same T-SQL surface, indexing, and transactional guarantees. But with one transformative capability:

Automatic replication to OneLake. When the meter registration system inserts a new meter into the SQL Database, that row is automatically, continuously replicated as a Delta table in OneLake. No ETL pipeline. No CDC configuration. No mirroring setup. The data just appears, queryable by Spark Notebooks, the Warehouse, Power BI DirectLake, and every other Fabric workload. This is the zero-ETL vision: operational data flows into analytics automatically.
Part 1: What Is SQL Database in Fabric?

SQL Database in Fabric is a fully managed SQL Server-compatible transactional database that runs inside a Fabric workspace. Under the hood, it is the same engine as Azure SQL Database with automatic tuning, intelligent query processing, and enterprise-grade reliability.

SQL Server engine — Full T-SQL support including stored procedures, triggers, user-defined functions, views, indexes (clustered, nonclustered, columnstore), constraints (PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, CHECK, UNIQUE, DEFAULT), and transaction isolation levels.

OLTP-optimized — Row-store engine optimized for single-row reads and writes, point lookups by primary key, and high-concurrency transactional workloads. The opposite of the Warehouse’s columnar scan engine.
Fully managed — Fabric handles provisioning, patching, backups, high availability, and scaling. You create a database and start writing T-SQL. No server management.
Automatic OneLake replication — Every table is continuously replicated to OneLake as Delta tables. Near-real-time latency (typically seconds). No configuration required.
Capacity-based billing — Compute is drawn from your Fabric capacity (CUs), just like Warehouses and Spark sessions. No separate Azure SQL pricing.
Meter Registration App / Work Order System / Field Crew Mobile App
│  INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE (OLTP — single rows, high frequency)
SQL Database in Fabric (SQL Server engine)
OneLake (Delta tables — same data, Delta format)
├──→ Warehouse (cross-database T-SQL joins with Gold star schema)
├──→ Spark Notebooks (ML feature engineering on meter attributes)
├──→ Power BI DirectLake (near-real-time work order dashboards)
└──→ Lakehouse shortcuts (reference data for Bronze/Silver pipeline)

SQL Database in Fabric is not a replacement for the Warehouse. It is not designed for large analytical scans, star schema joins, or data warehouse workloads. It is designed for transactional patterns: application backends, operational data stores, and any workload that requires row-level CRUD with strict ACID guarantees. The analytics happen downstream, via the automatic OneLake replication.
Part 2: Creating and Configuring a SQL Database
Navigate to your workspace, click “+ New item” → “SQL Database,” enter a name (sqldb_grid_operations), and click Create. The database is provisioned within seconds.

Once created, the database opens in a SQL editor identical to the Warehouse editor. You can also connect from SSMS, Azure Data Studio, any JDBC/ODBC client, Entity Framework, or application frameworks (Node.js mssql, Python SQLAlchemy, .NET SqlClient).
Automatic tuning — The engine monitors query performance and automatically creates, validates, and applies indexes. It identifies and reverts execution plan regressions.
Intelligent Query Processing — Adaptive joins, memory grant feedback, interleaved execution. These optimizations run transparently.
Automatic plan correction — When a query plan regresses, the engine automatically forces the last known good plan.
Auto-tuning means you spend less time manually creating indexes compared to traditional SQL Server. The engine learns from your query patterns and optimizes itself.
Part 3: Schema Design for Energy Operational Systems

OLTP schema design follows different principles than analytical star schema design. While the Warehouse uses wide fact tables with denormalized dimensions, the SQL Database uses normalized schemas (3NF) optimized for write performance and data integrity.
Energy Operational Schema: Meter Registration & Work Orders
meter_type       VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL CHECK (meter_type IN ('SMART', 'LEGACY', 'PREPAID')),
install_date     DATE NOT NULL DEFAULT CAST(GETUTCDATE() AS DATE),
-- Work order tasks (junction: one work order has multiple tasks)
work_order_id    INT NOT NULL REFERENCES dbo.work_orders(work_order_id),
-- Meter lookups by serial number (registration app)
-- Work orders by substation and status (crew assignment queries)
-- Work orders by crew (dashboard: my assigned work)

Important: In the Warehouse, you do not create indexes — the engine handles optimization automatically. In the SQL Database, indexes are essential for OLTP performance. Without them, point lookups degrade to table scans and your meter registration app slows to a crawl under load.
-- Lookup meter by serial number (registration app)
-- All active meters at a substation (field crew view)
-- Open work orders for a crew (mobile app dashboard)
-- Field crew completes a work order
-- Create a work order with tasks (atomic operation)
INSERT INTO dbo.work_orders (substation_id, priority, category, description)

Important: Transactions in the SQL Database use the SQL Server locking model (READ COMMITTED by default) with row-level locking for high concurrency. The Warehouse uses snapshot isolation. This difference matters when designing for concurrent application workloads where multiple field crews update work orders simultaneously.

This is the feature that makes SQL Database in Fabric transformative. Every table in the SQL Database is automatically and continuously replicated to OneLake as a Delta table. There is no configuration, no pipeline, no mirroring setup. It just works.
Change tracking — The SQL Server engine tracks changes at the row level. When an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE modifies data, the change is captured.
Delta conversion — Changes are continuously converted to Delta format and written to the database’s OneLake folder. Each table gets its own Delta table.
Near-real-time latency — Replication typically completes within seconds of the commit. For most workloads, the OneLake Delta table is less than a minute behind.
Schema synchronization — When you ALTER TABLE to add columns, the Delta table schema is updated automatically.
What Gets Replicated vs. What Does Not
The Zero-ETL Impact for Energy Operations

Consider the traditional architecture: the meter registration app writes to a SQL Server database. A nightly ETL pipeline extracts the data, transforms it, and loads it into a data warehouse. Analysts query the warehouse the next morning. Meter data is 12–24 hours stale.

With SQL Database in Fabric: the meter app writes to the SQL Database. The meter row appears in OneLake within seconds. The Warehouse can cross-query it immediately. Power BI shows the new meter on a DirectLake dashboard without waiting for a refresh. The work order dashboard reflects real-time field crew progress.

Warning: Automatic replication handles data movement, not transformation. If your analytics require cleaning, enriching, or reshaping the data (adding consumption bands, resolving surrogate keys, calculating costs), you still need a transformation layer. Zero-ETL means zero extraction and loading pipelines — not zero data engineering.
Part 6: Querying SQL Database from Other Workloads
The replicated Delta tables in OneLake are the integration bridge. Every Fabric workload can read SQL Database data through its preferred interface.
-- Cross-database query: join operational meter data with Gold star schema
ON m.meter_id = f.meter_sk  -- or join via business key
JOIN wh_grid_analytics.gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
# Option 1: Read replicated Delta table via Lakehouse shortcut
# (create a shortcut in lh_silver_grid pointing to sqldb's OneLake path)
# Option 2: Read via abfss:// path directly
Options 1 and 2 read the replicated Delta tables from OneLake — fast and with no impact on the SQL Database’s compute. This is the recommended approach for analytical queries on operational data.
DirectLake — Connect the semantic model to the replicated Delta tables. Near-real-time dashboard data with no import required. Recommended for most work order and meter status dashboards.
DirectQuery — Connect directly to the SQL Database engine via TDS. Queries hit the live database. Use only when you need absolute transactional consistency (not even seconds of lag).
SQL Database supports the full SQL Server programmability surface — significantly richer than the Warehouse.
Stored Procedure: Create Work Order with Validation
IF NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM dbo.meters WHERE substation_id = @substation_id)
THROW 50001, 'Substation not found in meter registry', 1;
SELECT TOP 1 @region = region FROM dbo.meters WHERE substation_id = @substation_id;
INSERT INTO dbo.work_orders (substation_id, priority, category, description,
@crew_id, CASE WHEN @crew_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'ASSIGNED' ELSE 'OPEN' END);
-- Call from the work order management app
-- Automatically set updated_at when meters table is modified
WHERE meter_id IN (SELECT meter_id FROM inserted);

Triggers are unique to the SQL Database — the Warehouse does not support them. Use triggers sparingly for cross-cutting concerns like audit timestamps. For complex business logic, prefer stored procedures which are easier to debug and test.
Part 8: The Three SQL Items — Complete Architecture
How They Work Together on the Energy Platform
Field Apps (register meters, manage work orders)
│  shortcut to SQL Database tables for meter metadata
│  + batch meter readings from SFTP, SCADA from historian
│  PySpark notebooks for dedup, cast, MERGE
│  Cross-database joins to sqldb for live meter attributes
│  Work order dashboard (DirectLake on SQL Database replicas)
│  Grid operations dashboard (DirectLake on Warehouse Gold)

The three SQL items each serve a distinct role: SQL Database handles transactional writes from field applications. The Lakehouse handles batch ingestion and Spark-driven transformations. The Warehouse hosts the Gold star schema with stored procedures and governed security. OneLake connects them all — data written by one item is readable by any other item, in seconds.
Part 9: Decision Framework — When to Use Which SQL Item
•  What SQL Database in Fabric is — SQL Server engine for OLTP workloads, fully managed, capacity-based billing, with automatic OneLake replication
•  OLTP schema design — normalized 3NF schemas for meter registration and work order management (meters, work_orders, work_order_tasks, field_crews) with constraints, defaults, and CHECK constraints
•  Indexes for OLTP — nonclustered indexes for point lookups (meter serial, substation, crew assignments); essential for performance unlike the Warehouse which auto-manages
•  CRUD operations — INSERT with SCOPE_IDENTITY, point lookups by primary key, UPDATE for status changes, transactions with BEGIN TRY/CATCH for atomic multi-table operations
•  Automatic OneLake replication — every table replicated as Delta in near-real-time; what gets replicated vs. what does not; zero-ETL for data movement (not transformation)
•  Cross-workload querying — Warehouse cross-database joins to SQL Database, Spark reads via shortcuts or abfss://, Power BI DirectLake on replicated tables
•  Stored procedures and triggers — usp_create_work_order with validation and auto-crew-assignment; triggers for auto-updating timestamps; triggers are SQL Database-only
•  The three SQL items compared — 9-capability comparison table; SQL Database for OLTP, Warehouse for analytical DML, Lakehouse endpoint for reads
•  Complete architecture — how SQL Database (field apps) + Lakehouse (batch pipeline) + Warehouse (Gold star schema) work together, connected through OneLake
•  Decision framework — 9-scenario table for choosing the right SQL item for each energy platform workload
•  Create a SQL Database (sqldb_grid_operations). Create the meters, work_orders, field_crews, and work_order_tasks tables with constraints and indexes.
•  Insert sample data: 10 meters, 3 field crews, 5 work orders with tasks. Use transactions for the work order + tasks inserts.
•  Create the stored procedure usp_create_work_order. Call it with EXEC and verify the auto-assigned crew and status.
•  Verify automatic replication: wait 30 seconds after inserting data, then check the Fabric portal — the replicated Delta tables should appear under the SQL Database item.
•  Write a cross-database query from your Warehouse that joins SQL Database meter data with Gold fact table meter readings.
•  Read the replicated meters table from a Spark Notebook via a Lakehouse shortcut. Confirm the data matches the SQL Database.
•  Create a trigger (trg_meters_update) that auto-sets updated_at on meter modifications. Update a meter’s status and verify the timestamp changed.
•  Connect to the SQL Database from SSMS or Azure Data Studio. Run a point lookup and observe the execution plan — verify that the nonclustered index is used.

In Module DW5, we turn to Medallion Architecture for SQL Warehouses — implementing Bronze/Silver/Gold layers entirely in T-SQL using schemas, stored procedures, and MERGE patterns. You will build a governed, T-SQL-native data pipeline without writing a single line of PySpark.

blocks.content: Bronze → Silver → Gold — Entirely in T-SQL

Module Objective: Implement the Bronze → Silver → Gold medallion pattern for an energy data warehouse using only T-SQL. Organize with schemas instead of separate Lakehouses, use COPY INTO for Bronze loading, stored procedures for Silver MERGE and Gold star schema builds, views for governed access, data quality checks in T-SQL, and SCD Type 2 with stored procedures — all without writing a single line of PySpark.

The DE track (Modules DE1–DE7) implemented the medallion architecture with Spark Notebooks: PySpark for Bronze loading, Spark SQL for Silver MERGE, and PySpark for Gold star schema builds. Every transformation was code in a Notebook, executed by the Spark engine.

This module implements the same architecture entirely in T-SQL. No Spark. No PySpark. No Notebooks. Instead, you use Warehouse schemas for layer separation, COPY INTO for Bronze ingestion, stored procedures for Silver and Gold transformations, views for governed analyst access, and Pipeline Stored Procedure Activities for orchestration. This is the SQL analyst’s medallion.

The principles are identical — progressive refinement from raw to business-ready, immutable Bronze, deduplicated Silver, modeled Gold, quality gates between layers. The tools are different. If your team thinks in T-SQL, this is your blueprint.

This module covers: the Warehouse medallion layout (schemas as layers), Bronze design with COPY INTO, Silver stored procedures with MERGE and deduplication, Gold star schema builds with surrogate keys and SCD Type 2 in pure T-SQL, aggregate views for Power BI, data quality checks in T-SQL, cross-database integration with Lakehouses, and a production orchestration pattern using Pipeline Stored Procedure Activities.

In the DE track, each medallion layer was a separate Lakehouse (lh_bronze_grid, lh_silver_grid, lh_gold_grid). In the SQL Warehouse approach, you use schemas within a single Warehouse to separate layers. Both are valid; the choice depends on your team’s operating model.
Option A: Schemas as Layers (Single Warehouse)
├── staging schema     ← Bronze equivalent: raw data lands here
├── silver schema      ← Cleansed: deduplicated, typed, validated
├── gold schema        ← Star schema: facts, dimensions, aggregates
└── audit schema       ← Quality logs, refresh history
Option B: Separate Warehouse per Layer

For larger teams or stricter security, create one Warehouse per layer: wh_staging (Bronze), wh_silver, wh_gold. Cross-database queries connect them. This mirrors the Lakehouse approach but with T-SQL write access at every layer.
The schema approach enables clean security boundaries:
-- ETL service: full access to staging and silver, read+write gold
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON SCHEMA::staging TO [ETLService];
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON SCHEMA::silver TO [ETLService];
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON SCHEMA::gold TO [ETLService];
Part 2: Bronze Layer — COPY INTO for Raw Ingestion

The Bronze layer’s contract is the same as in the DE track: land a faithful copy of source data, add metadata, and never overwrite. The T-SQL tool for this is COPY INTO — the fastest bulk-load mechanism in the Warehouse.
reading_date     VARCHAR(20),     -- land as string, cast in Silver
daily_kwh        VARCHAR(20),     -- land as string, cast in Silver

Schema-on-read principle: Bronze tables accept everything as VARCHAR. This prevents load failures if the source sends unexpected formats (e.g., 'N/A' in a numeric column). The Silver layer handles type casting and validation. This mirrors the DE track’s approach where Bronze CSVs are read with inferSchema=False.
-- Load today's meter CSV from the Lakehouse Files/ landing zone
COPY INTO parallelizes the read across files and uses bulk insert mechanics. For 2–5 million daily meter readings, it is significantly faster than row-by-row INSERT.
Wrapping Bronze Load in a Stored Procedure
-- Dynamic COPY INTO with parameterized path
INSERT INTO audit.refresh_log (table_name, load_date, row_count, status)
Part 3: Silver Layer — Stored Procedures for MERGE

The Silver layer’s contract: one trusted, deduplicated, type-conformant version of each entity. In T-SQL, this is implemented as a stored procedure that deduplicates, casts, validates, and MERGEs into the Silver table.
-- Step 1: Deduplicate + cast + validate in a CTE
WHEN TRY_CAST(daily_kwh AS DECIMAL(12,3)) IS NULL THEN 'MISSING'
AND TRY_CAST(reading_date AS DATE) IS NOT NULL
ON t.meter_id = s.meter_id AND t.reading_date = s.reading_date
WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN INSERT
INSERT INTO audit.refresh_log (table_name, load_date, row_count, status)

This single stored procedure replaces the entire Silver notebook from the DE track (nb_silver_meters). It performs deduplication (ROW_NUMBER), type casting (TRY_CAST), null handling (ISNULL), standardization (UPPER/TRIM), range validation, quality flagging, and MERGE — all in one parameterized T-SQL procedure. TRY_CAST is the T-SQL equivalent of PySpark’s safe cast: it returns NULL instead of failing on invalid values.
Part 4: Gold Layer — Star Schema in T-SQL
CASE WHEN DATEPART(WEEKDAY, d) IN (1, 7) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_weekend
SCD Type 2 Stored Procedure for Substations
SET effective_to = CAST(GETUTCDATE() AS DATE), is_current = 0
WHERE substation_id IN (SELECT substation_id FROM changes)
-- Step 3a: Insert new versions for changed substations
SELECT substation_id, substation_name, voltage_kv, region, status,
JOIN gold.dim_substations t ON s.substation_id = t.substation_id
JOIN gold.dim_meters m ON sm.meter_id = m.meter_id AND m.is_current = 1
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON sm.substation_id = s.substation_id AND s.is_current = 1
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON sm.reading_date = d.calendar_date
LEFT JOIN gold.dim_tariffs tr ON m.tariff_code = tr.tariff_code
ON t.meter_sk = src.meter_sk AND t.date_key = src.date_key
WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN INSERT
INSERT INTO audit.refresh_log (table_name, load_date, row_count, status)
Part 5: Gold Views for Governed Access
-- Monthly grid summary for executive dashboards
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON f.substation_sk = s.substation_sk
-- Substation health view for asset management

These views are the governed abstractions analysts query. Power BI connects to them via DirectLake. The billing team queries them via the SQL endpoint. The views encapsulate star schema join logic so analysts never need to write fact-dimension joins themselves.
Part 6: Data Quality Checks in T-SQL
SELECT @failures = COUNT(*) FROM silver.meters WHERE meter_id IS NULL;
INSERT INTO audit.quality_log (table_name, check_name, failures, total_rows,
SELECT @failures = COUNT(*) FROM silver.meters WHERE daily_kwh < 0;
INSERT INTO audit.quality_log (table_name, check_name, failures, total_rows,
INSERT INTO audit.quality_log (table_name, check_name, failures, total_rows,
-- Return result for Pipeline If Condition
SELECT CASE WHEN @has_critical_fail = 1 THEN 'FAIL' ELSE 'PASS' END AS gate_status;
The Pipeline calls audit.usp_quality_gate via a Stored Procedure Activity. The result (PASS/FAIL) drives an If Condition: PASS continues to Gold; FAIL triggers an alert and halts.

Many organizations run a hybrid architecture: data engineers use Lakehouses and Spark for heavy Bronze/Silver processing, while SQL analysts own the Gold layer in the Warehouse. Cross-database queries bridge the two.
-- Read Silver meters from a Lakehouse into Warehouse Gold
FROM lh_silver_grid.dbo.meters lh  -- Lakehouse via 3-part naming
JOIN gold.dim_meters m ON lh.meter_id = m.meter_id AND m.is_current = 1
JOIN gold.dim_substations s ON lh.substation_id = s.substation_id AND s.is_current = 1
JOIN gold.dim_date d ON lh.reading_date = d.calendar_date
LEFT JOIN gold.dim_tariffs tr ON m.tariff_code = tr.tariff_code

This pattern lets data engineers own Bronze/Silver in Lakehouses using PySpark, while SQL analysts own Gold in the Warehouse using T-SQL. Both read from OneLake; both write Delta. The cross-database query is the bridge.
Part 8: Production Orchestration with Pipeline Stored Procedure Activities
│   └── If Condition: gate_status = 'FAIL' → Alert + Fail Activity
└── Cleanup: DELETE FROM staging WHERE load_timestamp < -30 days

The entire medallion flow is a sequence of Pipeline Stored Procedure Activities calling Warehouse procedures. No Notebooks, no Spark sessions, no Python. The Pipeline orchestrates; the Warehouse transforms. Parameters flow from the Pipeline trigger through each stored procedure.

Each procedure logs to audit.refresh_log, creating a complete audit trail of every load: table name, date, row count, and status. The quality gate procedure returns a result set that the Pipeline evaluates with an If Condition.
Part 9: DE Track vs. DW Track Medallion — Comparison

Neither approach is superior. They produce the same result (Bronze → Silver → Gold star schema on Delta tables in OneLake) using different tools. The choice is about team skills. Many organizations use both: PySpark for heavy Bronze/Silver processing on high-volume data (50M meter readings), T-SQL stored procedures for Gold star schema builds and governed analytical views.
•  Warehouse medallion layout — schemas as layers (staging/silver/gold/audit) within a single Warehouse, with schema-level security for team separation
•  Bronze with COPY INTO — staging tables with VARCHAR columns (schema-on-read), bulk loading from OneLake files, parameterized Bronze load procedure with dynamic COPY INTO

•  Silver stored procedures — complete usp_merge_meters: deduplication (ROW_NUMBER), type casting (TRY_CAST), null handling (ISNULL), standardization (UPPER/TRIM), range validation, quality flagging, and MERGE — all in one procedure

•  Gold star schema in T-SQL — dim_date generation with GENERATE_SERIES, dim_substations with SCD Type 2 (IDENTITY surrogate keys, CTE for changes, expire/insert pattern), fact table build with surrogate key resolution and cost calculation
•  Views for governed access — v_monthly_grid_summary and v_substation_health as analyst-facing abstractions over the star schema
•  Data quality in T-SQL — usp_quality_gate procedure with null, negative, and duplicate checks, audit.quality_log table, PASS/FAIL result for Pipeline If Condition
•  Cross-database integration — reading Silver from Lakehouse into Warehouse Gold using three-part naming for hybrid architectures
•  Production orchestration — Pipeline with sequential Stored Procedure Activities: Bronze load → Silver MERGE → Quality gate → SCD2 → Fact build → Cleanup
•  DE vs. DW comparison — 9-point comparison table showing equivalent approaches in PySpark vs. T-SQL; neither is superior, choose by team skill
•  Create a Warehouse (wh_grid_analytics). Create schemas: staging, silver, gold, audit.
•  Create staging.raw_meter_readings with all-VARCHAR columns. Use COPY INTO to load a CSV from a Lakehouse Files/ section.
•  Create silver.meters with typed columns. Implement silver.usp_merge_meters: dedup, TRY_CAST, validate, MERGE. Run it and verify no duplicates in silver.meters.
•  Create gold.dim_date using GENERATE_SERIES. Create gold.dim_substations with SCD Type 2 columns. Implement gold.usp_scd2_substations. Simulate a voltage change and verify expire/insert.
•  Create gold.fact_meter_readings. Implement gold.usp_build_fact_meters with surrogate key resolution. Run it and verify fact rows join to dimensions.
•  Implement audit.usp_quality_gate with at least 3 checks. Verify it returns 'PASS'. Introduce a deliberate issue (insert a NULL meter_id into Silver) and verify it returns 'FAIL'.
•  Create gold.v_monthly_grid_summary and gold.v_substation_health views. Verify analysts can query them.
•  Build a Pipeline (pl_daily_grid_refresh) with Stored Procedure Activities: Bronze → Silver → Quality Gate → Gold. Add an If Condition on the quality gate result. Run the pipeline end-to-end.

In Module DW6, we cover Data Factory Pipelines for SQL Workloads — Copy Activity patterns for SQL sources, Stored Procedure Activity configuration, Script Activity for ad-hoc SQL, ForEach for multi-table processing, scheduling, error handling, and the master pipeline that automates the T-SQL medallion you built in this module.

rows: Schemas within one Warehouse (staging, silver, gold)
COPY INTO with load_timestamp and source_system columns
PySpark withColumn, filter, fillna, DeltaTable MERGE
T-SQL CTE with TRY_CAST, ISNULL, ROW_NUMBER, MERGE INTO
T-SQL MERGE with IDENTITY surrogate keys, INSERT...SELECT
T-SQL: CTE for changes, UPDATE expire, INSERT new versions
T-SQL usp_quality_gate with PASS/FAIL result set
