blocks.content: Data Factory — Pipelines for SQL Workloads

Module Objective: Master Fabric Data Factory Pipelines for SQL-centric workloads — Copy Activity for SQL and file sources, Stored Procedure Activity for calling Warehouse procedures, Script Activity for ad-hoc T-SQL, ForEach for multi-table processing, parameterization, scheduling, error handling, monitoring, and the master pipeline that automates the T-SQL medallion architecture from Module DW5.

Module DW5 built the complete T-SQL medallion: stored procedures for Bronze loading, Silver MERGE, Gold star schema builds, data quality gates, and SCD Type 2 processing. Every transformation is a stored procedure. But who calls those procedures? When do they run? What happens when one fails?

Fabric Data Factory Pipelines are the automation and orchestration layer. They are the scheduler, the sequencer, the error handler, and the monitor. Pipelines call your stored procedures in the right order, pass parameters (today’s date, the batch ID), check the quality gate result, branch on success or failure, retry on transient errors, and log everything to the Monitoring Hub.

The DE track (Module DE2) taught Pipelines with a data engineering lens: Copy Activity for file landing, Notebook Activities for Spark processing. This module teaches the same Pipeline engine with a SQL workload lens: Copy Activity for SQL sources, Stored Procedure Activity for calling Warehouse procedures, Script Activity for ad-hoc T-SQL, and ForEach for multi-table orchestration. Same engine, different activities, SQL-first mindset.

This module covers: Pipeline fundamentals for SQL analysts, Copy Activity for SQL and file sources, Stored Procedure Activity for Warehouse procedures, Script Activity for ad-hoc SQL, ForEach for multi-table processing, parameterization and dynamic content, error handling and retry, scheduling and triggers, monitoring, and the complete master pipeline for the energy grid medallion.
Part 1: Pipeline Fundamentals for SQL Workloads

A Pipeline is a Fabric item that defines a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of activities. Activities execute in sequence or in parallel based on dependency links. For SQL workloads, the three most important activities are:

Additional activities you will use: ForEach (loop over a list of tables or dates), If Condition (branch on quality gate result), Set Variable (capture values for downstream use), Web Activity (send alerts), Fail Activity (halt the pipeline with a descriptive error), and Execute Pipeline (call child pipelines).

Navigate to your workspace, click “+ New item” → “Data Pipeline,” enter a name (pl_daily_grid_refresh), and click Create. The Pipeline canvas opens with a toolbar of activities on the left and a visual canvas where you drag, drop, and connect activities.
Part 2: Copy Activity for SQL and File Sources
Pattern 1: SQL Source → Warehouse Staging Table
Copy energy pricing data from an Azure SQL Database (the trading system) directly into a Warehouse staging table:
•  Source: Azure SQL Database connector. Select the trading database, table dbo.hourly_prices. Add a query filter: WHERE price_date = @pipeline().parameters.load_date.
•  Destination: Fabric Warehouse connector. Select wh_grid_analytics, table staging.raw_energy_prices.
•  Write behavior: Append (add new rows) or Pre-copy script: TRUNCATE TABLE staging.raw_energy_prices (for full-refresh dimensions).
•  Mapping: Auto-map columns by name. Override individual mappings for column renames (source price_eur → destination price_mwh).
Pattern 2: File Source → Lakehouse Files/ (for COPY INTO)
Land meter CSV files from SFTP into the Lakehouse Files/ section, then let the Warehouse’s COPY INTO read them:
•  Source: SFTP or ADLS Gen2 connector. Path: /meters/{load_date}/*.csv. Use wildcard for multiple files.
•  Destination: Lakehouse connector. Target: lh_bronze_grid / Files/landing/meters/{load_date}/.
•  After the Copy Activity succeeds, a Stored Procedure Activity calls staging.usp_load_bronze_meters(@load_date) which runs COPY INTO on the landed files.
Pattern 3: REST API Source → Lakehouse Files/
Fetch weather forecast JSON from a REST API:
•  Source: REST connector. URL: https://api.weather.example.com/v2/forecast?region=NORTH&date={load_date}. Authentication: API key (stored in Key Vault, referenced via Pipeline Linked Service).
•  After landing, a Stored Procedure Activity calls the T-SQL JSON parsing procedure (DW3 Pattern) to flatten the JSON into silver.weather_forecasts.
•  Use parallel copy for large files: set Degree of Copy Parallelism > 1 for multi-gigabyte meter files.
•  Enable staging for cross-region copies: data routes through an intermediate staging area for better throughput.
•  Use Pre-copy script for idempotent loads: TRUNCATE TABLE staging.raw_energy_prices before each load ensures no duplicates from retries.
•  Set timeouts: 30 minutes for typical meter file copies, 60 minutes for large SCADA Parquet batches.
Part 3: Stored Procedure Activity — The SQL Workload Workhorse
The Stored Procedure Activity is the primary integration point between Pipelines and the Warehouse. It calls a stored procedure, passes parameters, and captures the output.
•  Warehouse: Select wh_grid_analytics from the dropdown.
•  Stored procedure: Select from the list (e.g., silver.usp_merge_meters).
•  Parameters: Map Pipeline parameters to procedure parameters. Example: @load_date = @pipeline().parameters.load_date.
•  Timeout: Set appropriate limits (e.g., 60 minutes for Silver MERGE on 2M meter rows).
The quality gate procedure (audit.usp_quality_gate) returns a result set with a gate_status column. To capture this in the Pipeline:
•  The Stored Procedure Activity output is accessed via @activity('Quality Gate').output.resultSets[0].rows[0].gate_status.
•  Use this in an If Condition: @equals(activity('Quality Gate').output.resultSets[0].rows[0].gate_status, 'PASS').
•  True path: continue to Gold activities. False path: Web Activity (send alert) + Fail Activity (halt pipeline with error message).
Part 4: Script Activity for Ad-Hoc T-SQL
The Script Activity executes a raw T-SQL statement against the Warehouse without needing a stored procedure. Use it for simple, one-off operations that do not warrant a stored procedure:
-- Script Activity: Truncate before full reload
-- Script Activity: Quick row count validation
SELECT COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM silver.meters

Script vs. Stored Procedure: Use Script Activity for simple statements (TRUNCATE, DELETE, quick SELECT). Use Stored Procedure Activity for reusable, parameterized, multi-step logic. If you write the same Script Activity in three pipelines, promote it to a stored procedure.
The ForEach activity loops over a collection and executes child activities for each item. For SQL workloads, this is essential for processing multiple tables with the same pattern.
Energy Example: Refresh All Silver Tables

The @{item()} expression evaluates to the current item in the loop: 'meters', then 'scada_telemetry', etc. With Sequential = false, all four Silver procedures run in parallel, significantly reducing total pipeline duration. With Sequential = true, they run one after another (useful when later tables depend on earlier ones).
Instead of hard-coding the table list, use a Lookup Activity to read it from a configuration table:

The Lookup Activity queries: SELECT procedure_name FROM audit.pipeline_config WHERE is_active = 1 ORDER BY run_order. The ForEach iterates over the result set. Adding a new Silver table means adding one row to the config table — no pipeline changes needed.
Define parameters at the pipeline level that flow through every activity:
Fabric Pipelines use expressions (similar to Azure Data Factory) for dynamic values:
// Weekday check for If Condition (skip pricing on weekends)
Part 7: Error Handling, Retry, and Alerting
Every dependency link between activities has a condition that controls when the downstream activity runs:
└── [On Failure] → Web Activity: Send Alert Email
└── Fail Activity: 'Silver MERGE failed for load_date='

The Fail Activity halts the pipeline with a descriptive error message that includes the load date and the actual error from the failed activity. This message appears in the Monitoring Hub and in alert emails, making troubleshooting immediate.
•  Copy Activities: Set retry count = 2, retry interval = 60 seconds. Handles transient SFTP connection failures.
•  Stored Procedure Activities: Set retry count = 1, retry interval = 120 seconds. Handles transient Warehouse compute throttling.
•  Script Activities: No retry (ad-hoc commands should not be retried blindly).

The Web Activity can call any HTTP endpoint: a Logic App for email alerts, a Teams webhook for chat notifications, or a custom API for ticket creation. Configure it on the failure path with a POST body that includes the pipeline name, run ID, failed activity, and error message.
The most common trigger for the energy grid pipeline: run daily at 02:00 AM UTC, after the overnight meter files have arrived:
•  Parameters: load_date = yesterday’s date (since files arrive overnight for the previous day’s readings). Expression: @formatDateTime(addDays(triggerBody().scheduledTime, -1), 'yyyy-MM-dd').
For sources that arrive unpredictably, trigger the pipeline when a file lands in OneLake:
•  Action: Run pl_daily_grid_refresh with load_date extracted from the file path.
Event-based triggers eliminate the need for scheduled polling. The pipeline runs exactly when new data arrives.
For backfilling historical data or re-running a failed load, execute the pipeline manually from the Fabric portal:
•  Click “Run” on the pipeline. Override parameters: load_date = '2026-02-15' for a specific backfill date.
•  For multi-day backfills, create a separate pl_backfill pipeline with a ForEach that iterates over a date range and calls pl_daily_grid_refresh for each date.
Part 9: Monitoring and the Monitoring Hub

The Fabric Monitoring Hub is the central dashboard for all pipeline runs. Every pipeline execution — triggered or manual — appears with: status (succeeded, failed, in progress, cancelled), start/end time and duration, activity-level breakdown (Gantt chart showing which activities ran, how long each took, and where failures occurred), input/output for each activity (parameters passed, rows copied, procedure results), and error details for failed activities.
What to Monitor for Energy Pipelines

For production energy pipelines, build a Power BI dashboard on the audit.refresh_log table that the stored procedures populate. This gives you: historical load volumes by date, processing duration trends, quality gate pass rates over time, and a clear view of which sources are deteriorating.
Here is the full master pipeline that automates the T-SQL medallion from DW5:
│   └── If Condition (weekday?): Copy Activity: SQL pricing → staging table
└── Script Activity: DELETE FROM staging WHERE load_timestamp < -30 days

This pipeline automates the entire energy grid medallion: 4 parallel ingestions land data, Bronze procedure loads files into staging, 4 parallel Silver procedures deduplicate and MERGE, the quality gate checks and halts if critical issues are found, Gold procedures build SCD2 dimensions and the fact table, and staging cleanup removes data older than 30 days. Every stored procedure logs to audit.refresh_log, and the Pipeline’s own run appears in the Monitoring Hub.
•  Pipeline fundamentals for SQL workloads — three key activities (Copy, Stored Procedure, Script) and when to use each
•  Copy Activity — SQL source to Warehouse staging, file source to Lakehouse Files/ for COPY INTO, REST API to landing zone; parallel copy and pre-copy scripts

•  Stored Procedure Activity — the workhorse for SQL pipelines: calling silver.usp_merge_meters, audit.usp_quality_gate, gold.usp_build_fact_meters; passing parameters; capturing result sets for quality gate logic
•  Script Activity — ad-hoc T-SQL for staging cleanup, TRUNCATE, quick validations; when to use vs. stored procedures
•  ForEach — parallel Silver MERGE across multiple tables; Lookup-driven configuration for adding tables without pipeline changes
•  Parameterization — load_date, batch_id, grid_region, run_mode; dynamic content expressions for file paths, error messages, weekday checks
•  Error handling — dependency conditions (Success/Failure/Completion/Skipped), error path pattern with Web Activity + Fail Activity, retry policies
•  Scheduling — daily CRON trigger at 02:00 AM, event-based trigger on file arrival, manual execution for backfills
•  Monitoring — Monitoring Hub for activity-level drill-down, 6-metric alert table, Power BI dashboard on audit.refresh_log
•  Master pipeline — complete pl_daily_grid_refresh: parallel ingestion → Bronze load → parallel Silver MERGE (ForEach) → Quality Gate (If Condition) → sequential Gold build → staging cleanup
•  Create a Pipeline (pl_daily_grid_refresh). Add pipeline parameters: load_date (String, default today), batch_id (String, default RunId).
•  Add a Copy Activity that lands a meter CSV file into a Lakehouse Files/landing/ folder. Parameterize the destination path with load_date.
•  Add a Stored Procedure Activity that calls staging.usp_load_bronze_meters with @pipeline().parameters.load_date.

•  Add a ForEach Activity with items = ['meters', 'scada_telemetry']. Inside the ForEach, add a Stored Procedure Activity calling silver.usp_merge_@{item()}. Set Sequential = false for parallel execution.
•  Add a Stored Procedure Activity calling audit.usp_quality_gate. Add an If Condition checking the gate_status result. On False (FAIL), add a Fail Activity with a descriptive error message.
•  Add Stored Procedure Activities for gold.usp_scd2_substations and gold.usp_build_fact_meters (sequential, after quality gate).
•  Add a Script Activity at the end that deletes staging data older than 30 days.
•  Set error paths on the Silver ForEach: on failure, connect to a Web Activity (or Set Variable) that captures the error message.
•  Create a scheduled trigger: daily at 02:00 AM UTC. Set load_date = yesterday’s date using @formatDateTime(addDays(triggerBody().scheduledTime, -1), 'yyyy-MM-dd').
•  Run the pipeline manually. Open the Monitoring Hub and examine the Gantt chart. Verify parallel Silver procedures ran concurrently. Check audit.refresh_log for log entries.

In Module DW7 (Capstone), you build the complete enterprise data warehouse end-to-end — from an empty Fabric workspace to a production-ready energy grid warehouse with ingestion, staging, Silver MERGE, quality gates, Gold star schema with SCD Type 2, governed views, Row-Level Security, and a fully automated master pipeline. Everything you have learned in this track comes together in one integrated project.
